sanskrit_devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः ।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति ॥ १५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ |
śāntiṁ nirvāṇa-paramāṁ mat-saṁsthām adhigacchati || 15 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Thus practicing constant control of the body, mind and activities, the mystic transcendentalist, his mind regulated, attains to the kingdom of God [or the abode of Kṛṣṇa] by cessation of material existence.

Thus practicing constant control of the body, mind and activities, the mystic transcendentalist, his mind regulated, attains to the kingdom of God [or the abode of Krishna] by cessation of material existence.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2166 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The ultimate goal in practicing yoga is now clearly explained. Yoga practice is not meant for attaining any kind of material facility; it is to enable the cessation of all material existence.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.15 Yunjan, concentrating; atmanam, the mind; evam, thus, according to the methods shown above; sada, for ever; the yogi, niyata-manasah, of controlled mind; adhi-gacchati, achieves; santim, the Peace, the indifference to worldly attachments and possessions; nirvana-paramam, which culminates in

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.15 ‘Ever applying his mind on Me,’ i.e., the Supreme Brahman, the Supreme Person and the holy and auspicious object of meditation, ‘the Yogin of controlled mind,’ i.e., one having his mind steady because of his being purified in mind through contact with Me, comes to the peace which abides in Me,

disagreements_among_translators.word: nirvāṇa-paramām mat-saṁsthām (peace of nirvāṇa abiding in Me)

positions.rendering: the kingdom of God [or the abode of Krishna] by cessation of material existence
Peace, the indifference to worldly attachments and possessions, which culminates in Liberation, and abides in Me
the peace which abides in Me, which is of the highest degree of beatitude
the supreme bliss which commences for an embodied being after the cessation of birth and death

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Across traditions the compound nirvāṇa-paramām mat-saṁsthām is read as terminal liberation abiding in Krishna/Brahman. Engineering analog cannot reach this destination; STRETCHED honestly.

literal_meaning: Thus continuously yoking the self, the yogi of disciplined mind attains the peace culminating in nirvāṇa that abides in Me.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.15 names the result of sustained meditation: the disciplined yogi attains śāntim nirvāṇa-paramām mat-saṁsthām — peace culminating in nirvāṇa, abiding in Krishna. Shankara reads nirvāṇa-paramām as 'culminating in Liberation' and mat-saṁsthām as 'abiding in Me'. Ramanuja reads the same compound as 'the peace which abides in Me, which is of the highest degree of beatitude.' Across traditions the verse is the chapter's first clear naming of the meditative end-state and is explicitly theistic — peace abides in Krishna. The engineering analog must be honestly STRETCHED.
